mnemonics.title: Stream Class Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: I O IO F - ios_base → ios → istream/ostream → iostream → fstream. Remember: 'I Own IO Files' - I (ios_base) Own (ios) IO (istream/ostream/iostream) Files (fstream/ifstream/ofstream)

mnemonics.title: File Stream Classes
mnemonics.content: i = input (read), o = output (write), f = full (read+write). ifstream=read, ofstream=write, fstream=both. Think: 'i for in, o for out, f for full control'

acronyms.usage: Refers to the C++ I/O system built on stream classes
Marker indicating no more data to read from a stream or file
Operations supported by different stream classes - ifstream=R, ofstream=W, fstream=R+W

visualTips.visualization: Think of it as an inverted tree: ios_base at root, branches split into istream (left) and ostream (right), merge at iostream (center), file streams branch off at the top. Visual: Root(ios_base) → Trunk(ios) → Branches(istream/ostream) → Crown(iostream) → Leaves(ifstream/ofstream/fstream)
Imagine a water reservoir (streambuf) between a faucet (ostream) and a drain (file/screen). Water (data) flows through the buffer, allowing efficient batched transfers rather than drop-by-drop.
Think of file modes as door types: in=entrance, out=exit, app=keyhole at end, trunc=demolition crew, binary=no translation booth.

analogies.analogy: Think of a stream like a conveyor belt and the buffer as the loading dock. The stream (conveyor) moves data continuously, while the buffer (loading dock) holds chunks for efficient transfer.
ios_base is like a blueprint/abstract specifications (formats, flags, base capabilities), while ios is like the actual building built on that blueprint adding connection to physical devices.

eof() is like a 'stop sign ahead' signal - it tells you the road ends AFTER you've passed the last visible marker. fail() is like an 'accident occurred' - something went wrong with the operation itself.
